title: The Premiere

jokes.text: I hope I'm not paying you by the word.
jokes.setup: Alexis's publicist opens with an extremely jargon-heavy, buzzword-laden pitch: 'optimize a branded approach to real-time engagement with your fanbase through contextual marketing.'
jokes.punchline: Moira cuts through it with a perfectly dry one-liner implying the publicist's verbosity is financially dangerous.
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira wit — devastating economy, perfect character voice, and it lands because the setup earns it so thoroughly. The jargon is so overblown that the punchline feels inevitable yet still satisfying. Moira's disdain for pretension reads as hypocritical given her own verbal excesses, which adds an ironic layer.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm essentially a multi-platform digital strategist who leverages synergistic brand optimization through targeted stakeholder engagement and strategic narrative dissemination across diversified mediums.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Do they pay you by the word?

jokes.text: 'Crows.' 'Movie.' 'Premiere?!' — The flip-chart reveal
jokes.setup: Alexis's publicist builds dramatic tension reading words off a flip-chart: 'Crows.' 'Movie.' Everyone reacts with increasing alarm at the word 'Premiere.'
jokes.punchline: It's just Alexis's plan for a local screening — the dramatic build-up was completely disproportionate to the modest idea.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection works well — the one-word-at-a-time flip-chart format and escalating reactions set up an expectation of something grand, only to reveal a small-town screening. Good structural comedy but relies on a fairly standard comedic device.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We need to really focus on ideation for the following idea.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, why are you even involved in this conversation?
jokes.setup: David has been participating in the conversation about Alexis's publicity plans.
jokes.punchline: Moira dismisses David entirely, highlighting her selective awareness of who she deems relevant.
jokes.explanation: Very Moira — her dismissal of family members as irrelevant is a reliable character beat. Sharp and efficient but not her most original deployment of this trait.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, you just said you were going to focus on sustainable sourcing, but then you also said you didn't care about where the products came from.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, well, why are you even involved in this? This is my thing.

jokes.text: Oh, sure, for a scrappy little novice, but not for someone who's cut many a crimson rug.
jokes.setup: Alexis pitches a local premiere screening as a 'cute press angle.'
jokes.punchline: Moira rejects it by invoking her self-image as a seasoned red-carpet veteran — 'cut many a crimson rug' being a characteristically Moira-esque phrase for 'walked many red carpets.'
jokes.explanation: 'Cut many a crimson rug' is peak Moira-speak — an extravagantly baroque way of saying something simple. The character integration score is high because this exact phrasing could only come from Moira. Highly quotable as a specimen of her linguistic register.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A local premiere? Well, I have certainly cut many a crimson rug in my day.

jokes.text: What's next, I sign my name in the pavement outside Bob's Garage? Jocelyn gives you an honourary degree at her school? Host the Met Ga-la at David's store?
jokes.setup: Moira is dismissing Alexis's local-premiere idea as beneath her stature.
jokes.punchline: She escalates through a rapid-fire list of increasingly absurd small-town equivalents of prestige Hollywood events, culminating in the Met Gala at a general store.
jokes.explanation: Strong escalation structure — each item in the list is funnier than the last, and 'Met Ga-la' (with Moira's pronunciation affectation implied) is a great button. The specificity of 'Bob's Garage' grounds it in the show's world. The list format is efficient comedy writing that rewards the whole cast setup.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I could be honored at the opening of a new parking garage. They could name a parking space after me. They could paint my face on the pavement.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Or perhaps they'll host a gala in my honor. A grand, elegant affair.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Actually, they could host the Met Gala at David's store.

jokes.text: I don't like that one.
jokes.setup: Moira has just listed 'Host the Met Ga-la at David's store' as an absurd hypothetical.
jokes.punchline: David quietly objects to specifically that one — not the general argument, just the idea that his store would host the Met Gala.
jokes.explanation: Four words, maximum effect. David's objection reveals his pride in the store — he minds being lumped in with small-town mediocrity. The deadpan delivery and perfect economy make this an excellent reaction beat. Classic David self-awareness that only he could deliver.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The Met Gala? At my store? Mother, my store is 800 square feet. The Met Gala is in New York. And I don't have the infrastructure, the clientele, or frankly, the will to host that event.

jokes.text: Roland has a guy who rents out live crows.
jokes.setup: Alexis is pitching her premiere idea as having real resources — Ronnie for the carpet.
jokes.punchline: The crowning detail: Roland apparently has access to a live crow rental service, which is both absurd and weirdly specific to Roland.
jokes.explanation: Functionally also foreshadows the crow catastrophe at the end, giving this throwaway line rewatch value. The absurdity of a crow rental business is perfectly calibrated — strange enough to be funny, specific enough to feel real. Earns points for planting plot and comedy simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm having a premiere party for my movie, and I need everything to be absolutely perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I happen to have a contact who rents crows.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why would you have a contact who rents crows?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You'd be surprised how often that comes up.

jokes.text: I cannot stop the masses from gathering to view the film, but best... best to avoid any fulsome indecorum until we're assured a positive reaction.
jokes.setup: Moira pretends to take the diplomatic high road about the premiere.
jokes.punchline: Her elaborate Moira-speak ('fulsome indecorum') reveals she's primarily worried about being humiliated if the film is bad — self-interest dressed up as magnanimity.
jokes.explanation: Solid Moira character work — her vocabulary ('fulsome indecorum') doing the heavy lifting — but the joke is somewhat buried in the diplomatic hedging. Functional rather than exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I once attended a premiere on a glass-bottomed pirate ship for a Waterworld sequel.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A glass-bottomed pirate ship? For a Waterworld sequel?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, it was very on-brand for the studio at the time.

jokes.text: Remember that 'Waterworld II' premiere? ...the last place anyone wanted to be when those reviews came in was a glass-bottomed pirate ship in the middle of the ocean.
jokes.setup: Moira cautions against holding a premiere before knowing audience reaction.
jokes.punchline: She illustrates her point with the spectacularly specific anecdote of a 'Waterworld II' premiere on a glass-bottomed pirate ship — implying she was there for a catastrophic critical reception.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Waterworld II' is already funny as a concept (implying Waterworld was bad enough to warrant a sequel nobody wanted), but the glass-bottomed pirate ship is a deeply specific, absurdist detail that feels like real Hollywood excess gone wrong. The image of being stranded at sea when terrible reviews arrive is vividly uncomfortable and hilarious. Strong character integration — only Moira would have this reference in her personal history. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Good luck.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Thank you, I'm going to need all the luck I can get.

jokes.text: Lick rust! Thank you!
jokes.setup: Moira says 'Good luck though' to Alexis's premiere idea with obvious insincerity.
jokes.punchline: Alexis mishears or misprocesses 'good luck' and responds with what appears to be a garbled version — 'Lick rust!' — treating it as if it were a well-wishing phrase before catching herself with 'Thank you!'
jokes.explanation: A delightfully weird Alexis moment — whether she's quoting some actress-world superstition phrase or simply garbling words, it's perfectly in-character for her to say something completely unhinged with total confidence before recovering. The 'Thank you!' pivot suggests she may have caught her own oddness. Quirky and distinctive.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I don't know what's wrong with my car.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's dead. It's completely dead. I would need to examine it more closely, but I'm fairly certain the car is deceased.

jokes.text: I do. It's a death trap that needs to be set on fire and pushed off a cliff.
jokes.setup: Patrick says he doesn't know what's going on with his car.
jokes.punchline: Stevie responds with complete certainty and a disproportionately violent assessment of the vehicle.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's bluntness is her calling card, and 'set on fire and pushed off a cliff' is a satisfyingly specific escalation. The structure (Patrick's uncertainty vs. Stevie's absolute certainty) is clean. Not groundbreaking but well-executed character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Apparently being maid of honor now includes dental appointments. I didn't realize that was in the job description, but here we are.

jokes.text: ...according to David, driving his fiancé to go get his wisdom teeth out is totally within my maid-of-honour responsibilities.
jokes.setup: Stevie explains why she's driving Patrick to the dentist.
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of 'maid-of-honour responsibilities' including dental chauffeur duty — and her deadpan delivery implies she finds this equally absurd but has accepted it.
jokes.explanation: Efficiently establishes that David has weaponized the maid-of-honour role for personal convenience, which is classic David. Stevie's resigned acceptance is funny. Solid character comedy that does double duty establishing situation and tone.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's great that you're embracing the whole... rustic charm thing. Not everyone can pull off living in a motel room. Most people would find it depressing, but you seem to have a real knack for making the best of it.

jokes.text: Okay, tell me how I'm being unreasonable?
jokes.setup: Stevie has just explained that she's been conscripted as a dental chauffeur per David's definition of maid-of-honour duties.
jokes.punchline: David's genuine indignation and request for validation — completely unaware of how unreasonable he's being — is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A reliable David beat — his complete lack of self-awareness about his own demands. Works well but is well-trodden character territory by season 6.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I just thought by now I'd have a house, a car, maybe even a kid.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're not having a kid?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, eventually, maybe. Why?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know, you just seem like you could use one.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's... I'm not sure how to take that.

jokes.text: I guess I just feel like after making such a big deal out of wanting to see what else is out there, isn't it kind of... pathetic to like... crawl back? Don't answer that.
jokes.setup: Stevie is confessing her anxiety about returning to the motel job after making a show of leaving.
jokes.punchline: She asks a vulnerable question and then immediately, reflexively shuts it down — 'Don't answer that' — before anyone can confirm her fear.
jokes.explanation: Very Stevie — the self-protective instinct to retract vulnerability the moment it's exposed. The comedic structure (ask for validation, immediately deny the request) is clean and characterful. Works on both comic and emotional levels, which is Schitt's Creek's signature.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: And now, the woman we've all been waiting for... Moira Rose!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that's very kind of you to say, though I wouldn't call it a 'pawky' introduction.

jokes.text: Look at me. Two years ago, I thought I'd have a wife and kid at this point. Yeah, well, one out of two isn't bad.
jokes.setup: Patrick offers Stevie perspective: 'Look at me. Two years ago, I thought I'd have a wife and a kid at this point.'
jokes.punchline: Stevie instantly: 'Yeah, well, one out of two isn't bad.' — a quip that acknowledges Patrick's engagement while gently clowning his missed expectations.
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline with excellent economy. The joke has a warm edge — acknowledging David is the 'wife' (or the equivalent) without belaboring it. Stevie's comic timing is baked into the line's brevity. Solid.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A dress from 'The Dress Barn'? Jocelyn, that's not a store, that's a zoning violation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And it's not a gala, it's a county fair ribbon-cutting ceremony.

jokes.text: I've been told I'm... very young at heart.
jokes.setup: Stevie noted Patrick only achieved 'one out of two' of his life goals (no kid). Patrick tries to spin the 'no kid' part.
jokes.punchline: His self-justification — 'I've been told I'm very young at heart' — works as a joke because it's both a humble self-deprecation and a deflection of the baby conversation.
jokes.explanation: A pleasant small beat — the hesitation before 'very young at heart' signals self-awareness. Functional but not remarkable.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh my gosh, I got an autograph from a celebrity!

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: But wait, they spelled their name completely differently than how it's actually spelled.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: So now I have an autograph from someone who isn't actually them.

jokes.text: Can you drive better, please?
jokes.setup: Patrick and Stevie have been having a genuine, warm conversation about life goals. David has apparently been listening from the backseat.
jokes.punchline: David's contribution to this emotional moment is a complaint about Stevie's driving — deflecting from the conversation's sentiment with banal selfishness.
jokes.explanation: David's interruption of a sincere moment with a mundane complaint is a perfect character beat. The timing is everything — a punctuation mark that deflates warmth without destroying it. Economy is near-perfect: three words that say everything about David.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, of course bird scientists would be the ultimate movie critics. They have the best perspective on everything.

jokes.text: Hello, Access Hollywood? Have I got a star sighting for you! Very pawky, Jocelyn.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn excitedly greets Moira by pretending to call a celebrity gossip show about her presence.
jokes.punchline: Moira's response — 'Very pawky, Jocelyn' — is a perfectly condescending dismissal using an archaic word for 'dryly humorous' that signals she is not amused.
jokes.explanation: 'Pawky' is a Moira word — obscure, faintly insulting, and delivered as if conferring the mildest compliment. Jocelyn's enthusiasm vs. Moira's studied coolness is a reliable dynamic. Character integration is strong.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Good fun. So it's good, but not great. Fun, but not hilarious.

jokes.text: It reminds me of that time that Renée Zellweger's brother came in here, and I got him to sign a menu. I mean, he spelled his name differently, but I swear it was him.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is excited about the premiere as a celebrity event for the town.
jokes.punchline: Her biggest celebrity encounter is getting a signed menu from a man who may or may not be Renée Zellweger's brother — who spelled his own name differently.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed small-town story. The specificity of 'Renée Zellweger's brother' (not Renée herself, not even a named celebrity), combined with the admission that he spelled his name differently (suggesting it wasn't him at all), builds to a perfectly deflating conclusion. Jocelyn's insistence — 'I swear it was him' — is the button. Charming and very specific to the show's world.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We were very careful not to make an issues movie.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So I'm thrilled the reviewer found such rich social commentary about the plight of crows.

jokes.text: Ornithology Today! Well, if anyone knows crows...
jokes.setup: The first review of 'The Crows Have Eyes III' has been posted — from Ornithology Today.
jokes.punchline: Moira's delighted recognition that a bird science journal is the relevant authority for her film — delivered with complete earnestness.
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of treating a bird-watching journal as the premiere film criticism source is heightened by Moira's sincerity. The phrase 'if anyone knows crows' as a credibility statement is quietly hilarious. Efficient and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Delightfully unhinged. That's exactly what I was going for.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Moira, that was a backhanded compliment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Was it? Because I'm choosing to hear it as high praise. Delightfully unhinged. It's rather fabulous, actually.

jokes.text: 'The Crows Have Eyes III is good fun.' Well, that's hyperbolic. I mean, they already had 'fun' and now they've added 'good.'
jokes.setup: The ornithology review calls the film 'good fun.'
jokes.punchline: Moira interprets the entirely ordinary phrase 'good fun' as an escalation — as if 'good' is a meaningful upgrade from mere 'fun' — which reveals her desperate hunger for validation.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Moira moment. The joke works on multiple levels: (1) she's parsing the phrase 'good fun' as if it were literary criticism, (2) her logic — that 'good' is an add-on to 'fun' — is absurd but follows its own internal rules, (3) her self-delusion is both funny and poignant. This is deeply character-specific — only Moira's particular combination of narcissism, insecurity, and verbal precision would produce this reading. One of the episode's strongest moments.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: My journalist friend is coming to the event.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose I'll have to make myself available as a bombshell attendee.

jokes.text: We were resolute about not making an issues movie, but I'm glad they picked up on that.
jokes.setup: The review praises the film for showcasing 'the emotional depth and intellectual capacity of our oft-maligned friend, the crow.'
jokes.punchline: Moira claims they were 'resolute' about NOT making an issues movie — but then immediately takes credit for the reviewer picking up on exactly that subtext.
jokes.explanation: Moira's self-contradicting logic — denying intent while claiming credit — is classic. The absurdity of a crow horror movie as an 'issues film' is also quietly funny. Her total lack of awareness of the contradiction is the engine.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: If you're going to watch this, I suggest you do so with the sound off and subtitles on. That way you can at least pretend you're reading something intellectually stimulating rather than watching a two-hour advertisement for a lifestyle you'll never have.

jokes.text: 'Moira Rose, as Dr. Clara Mandrake, is delightfully unhinged.' Do you know what they call this, gals? An understated rave.
jokes.setup: The review describes Moira's performance as 'delightfully unhinged.'
jokes.punchline: Moira reframes this mild-to-mildly-negative descriptor as 'an understated rave' — a creative redefinition that protects her ego.
jokes.explanation: 'Understated rave' is now a quotable Schitt's Creek phrase — a perfect Moira coinage that reframes 'unhinged' as a compliment. The character integration is nearly perfect: Moira's self-serving interpretation of criticism is her defining trait, and 'gals' — her attempt at peer camaraderie — is an extra layer. Very high quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, it's perfectly fine. The film completely ignores its own continuity anyway.

jokes.text: Well, you might want to tell your friend Janet that there just may be a bombshell attendee after all.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn mentions that a journalist would have loved to attend the premiere.
jokes.punchline: Moira reverses her entire position — she was just refusing to do the premiere — the moment she hears press will be involved.
jokes.explanation: The reversal is predictable but executed with flair — 'bombshell attendee' referring to herself is perfectly Moira. The joke is more about character revelation than structural surprise, but it lands.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you, Patrick. Thank you for that. Because what I really needed today was to get completely lost following directions from someone who's still half-unconscious from dental anesthesia.

jokes.text: Oh, my default is always formal, but I'm sure no one will bat a side-eye at black-tie casual.
jokes.setup: Someone expresses concern about not having anything fancy enough to wear to the premiere.
jokes.punchline: Moira's casual-yet-precise distinction between 'formal' (her default) and 'black-tie casual' — as if that's a universally understood dress code — is characteristically Moira.
jokes.explanation: 'Bat a side-eye' is a delicious Moira-ism (mixing 'bat an eyelid' with 'side-eye'), and 'black-tie casual' as a genuine dress code advice is the kind of out-of-touch guidance only Moira provides. Character integration is very strong.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm hungry.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: The wheels on the bus go round and round, round and round, round and round...

jokes.text: Oh, but do pack eyedrops! The film's first third is densely packed with exposition, and you won't want to blink.
jokes.setup: Moira is warmly encouraging people to attend the premiere.
jokes.punchline: Her unsolicited warning that the movie's first act is so exposition-heavy you shouldn't blink is a backhanded admission that it's a slog — delivered with complete sincerity as if it's a compliment.
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly self-undermining — Moira believes she's enthusiastically endorsing the film but is actually warning people it's dense and hard to watch. 'Pack eyedrops' as a film recommendation is a wonderful absurd detail. Strong character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You have the most beautiful skin I've ever seen.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I do have a very rigorous skincare routine, but it's not a big deal.

jokes.text: Oh, it's actually best [that you haven't seen the first two]. A lot of this film blatantly contradicts what's been established in the first two.
jokes.setup: Someone worries they haven't seen the first two 'Crows' films.
jokes.punchline: Moira reassures them that the third film 'blatantly contradicts' the first two — meant as comfort, but actually a damning admission about the film's quality and continuity.
jokes.explanation: Another Moira self-underminer. 'Blatantly contradicts' is a specific, legal-sounding phrase for what's essentially a criticism of the film's internal logic. Her matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier — she has no idea she's throwing the movie under the bus. Consistent with the episode's running joke of Moira being the film's worst advocate.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, there's something I've been wanting to talk to you about. Something I think could really change our lives.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Patrick, not here.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I think we should do it soon. I've been thinking about it constantly.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick!

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I want to have a baby.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: That was a fun car ride. Yes, it was very fun.
jokes.setup: They've arrived at the dentist/home after what was presumably an uncomfortable ride.
jokes.punchline: Both Patrick and David confirm it was 'fun' with a flatness that signals the exact opposite.
jokes.explanation: A functional bit of shared irony between the couple but doesn't rise above standard sitcom sarcasm. Serves as a transitional beat more than a genuine joke.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I've been thinking about it for a while now. I think I want to have a baby.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: Thank you so much for all the hot tips you were giving our Uber driver. For a second, I was concerned about my rating, but I think he seemed to enjoy all the wrong directions you were yelling at him.
jokes.setup: They've arrived from a presumably chaotic car ride. David apparently was yelling directions at the Uber driver.
jokes.punchline: Patrick sardonically recaps David's backseat behavior — concern for his Uber rating is a contemporary, relatable touch — while noting the directions were wrong.
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy that lets us infer a whole offscreen scene of David being insufferable in an Uber. The Uber rating anxiety is a relatable modern detail. Moderately effective.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You'd be a great father.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I can barely take care of myself. I once forgot to water my plants for three months and cried when they died.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That just means you have a capacity for love.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It means I'm negligent and emotionally unstable.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I wish you'd have my baby.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not how biology works, and also, no.

jokes.text: ♪ I'm a hungry, hungry hippo
jokes.setup: Patrick says he's hungry after the dentist procedure.
jokes.punchline: Instead of just saying 'I'm hungry,' he sings the 'hungry hungry hippos' jingle — presumably while gesturing or being physically affected by the anesthetic.
jokes.explanation: Post-anesthesia uninhibited Patrick is charming. The specific choice of 'hungry hungry hippo' as the song he defaults to is a nice character reveal — there's something sweet and slightly unhinged about it. Works well in context.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, Stevie, I really admire how you have such a carefree attitude about... tidiness.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Actually, I spent three hours cleaning this place.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, then you have a very clean carefree attitude about tidiness.

jokes.text: Can you make me a lasagna, David? Imagine? No.
jokes.setup: Patrick, hungry and loopy on anesthetic, asks David to make lasagna.
jokes.punchline: David's flat 'Imagine? No.' — the 'Imagine?' being a particularly clipped, dismissive Schitt's Creek verbal tic — is a perfect one-word-expanded rejection.
jokes.explanation: 'Imagine? No.' has become a David Rose signature phrase in the show. The economy is exceptional — 'Imagine?' alone does the work of 'Can you imagine me doing that? Obviously not.' It's deeply character-specific and one of those lines fans quote. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie, we're entering an exciting new phase. We're talking expansion, we're talking empire building, we're talking about acquiring a second property.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You bought another motel?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're not just 'buying' anything, Stevie. We're strategically positioning ourselves in the marketplace. This is about portfolio diversification, about leveraging our brand equity across multiple revenue streams.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's a motel two towns over, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's a *gateway* motel, Stevie. It's the beginning of something extraordinary.

jokes.text: Why are you so handsome? / It's a nine-step regimen I do twice a day. It's not a big deal.
jokes.setup: Patrick, loopy on anesthesia, compliments David: 'Why are you so handsome?'
jokes.punchline: David takes the question completely literally and gives a detailed answer about his skincare routine — 'a nine-step regimen I do twice a day. It's not a big deal.' — as if this is a normal response to a loopy compliment.
jokes.explanation: Excellent David joke. The setup (loopy Patrick's earnest compliment) creates a warm moment; the punchline (David's literal, detailed, slightly smug response about his skincare routine) is perfect character comedy. 'It's not a big deal' — delivered with full knowledge that it IS a big deal to David — is the button. Only David would respond to a romantic compliment with a skincare explanation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We need to sit down and talk business.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But not on your bed. I don't want to sit on your bed.

jokes.text: You know what I really wanna do with you? Uh-huh. Ethically speaking, I don't think we can do that with you in this state. Have a baby.
jokes.setup: Patrick says 'You know what I really wanna do with you?' in a suggestive tone; David responds as if the answer is sexual: 'Ethically speaking, I don't think we can do that with you in this state.'
jokes.punchline: Patrick's actual answer: 'Have a baby.' — an entirely innocent revelation that makes David's ethical concern about consent both retroactively funny and now newly alarming.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structural jokes. The misdirection is perfectly set up — the suggestive phrasing combined with the ethical-consent response creates a specific expectation. The punchline 'Have a baby' works on three levels: (1) it's completely innocent, undermining David's reading; (2) it's actually emotionally significant in the show's context; (3) David's ethical pre-emptive refusal now looks simultaneously righteous and silly. The structure is clean and the character dynamics serve the joke perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie, I've been thinking about us, about the business, about our future together.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And I've realized that what I really want is to buy you out of the Rosebud Motel.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You... you want to buy me out?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes. I think it's the best move for both of us.

jokes.text: That's a... that's a big one-eighty. / Have we met?
jokes.setup: Patrick has just said he wants to have a baby with David — a significant emotional revelation from someone David was convinced didn't want kids.
jokes.punchline: David's response: 'Have we met?' — implying he can't believe this is the same Patrick he knows, while also being a deflection of the emotional weight.
jokes.explanation: 'Have we met?' is an economical way to say 'this doesn't sound like you.' Works as both deflection and character beat. Good economy, slightly expected.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, Stevie, your poker face is slipping. I can tell you're absolutely thrilled about this book.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm not thrilled.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you're doing a wonderful job of hiding it.

jokes.text: I wish you were my dad.
jokes.setup: The conversation has drifted — Patrick has been praising David, including that he'd be a great dad.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's anesthesia spiral takes him from 'you'd be a great dad' to 'I wish you were MY dad' — a complete and hilarious non sequitur that crosses into Oedipal territory.
jokes.explanation: Spectacular. The escalation from 'you'd be a good dad' to 'I wish you were MY dad' — addressed to his fiancé — is a perfectly absurd anesthesia-logic leap. It's unexpected, slightly disturbing, very funny, and David's response ('now we're just spiraling off in all directions') is the perfect button. High quotability and rewatch value. One of the episode's standout moments.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I have a problem.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh sweetie, is it your skin? Because I have a dermatologist who can work miracles. Or your hair? I know someone in Toronto who specializes in damage control. We could do a weekend trip.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, Mom, it's not that.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, then it must be your nails. Or possibly your entire aura.

jokes.text: Okay, now we're just spiraling off in all directions.
jokes.setup: Patrick has just told David 'I wish you were my dad.'
jokes.punchline: David's calm diagnosis of the situation — 'spiraling off in all directions' — is a clinical understatement of what is a deeply weird conversation.
jokes.explanation: Good button on the 'wish you were my dad' escalation. David's measured language for a very unmeasured situation is characteristic. Works as a punchline reaction more than a standalone joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I was planning a birthday soirée for a very prominent socialite, and naturally, everything had to be absolutely flawless. I had secured a private jet to transport the guests, but wouldn't you know it, someone died on the flight.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But did that stop me? No. I simply had the body removed at the next stop, refreshed my lipstick, and we were back on schedule by appetizers.

jokes.text: David? I'm very hungry.
jokes.setup: After the deeply emotional/weird baby conversation, there's a moment of apparent tenderness as Patrick asks David to lie down with him.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's first words after this sweet moment: 'I'm very hungry.' — the anesthetic fog reasserts itself immediately.
jokes.explanation: A callback to the hunger theme that deflates the gentle moment nicely. The juxtaposition of emotional intimacy with 'I'm very hungry' is a reliable structure. Good use of the running gag.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's my dress!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, the shipping company labeled it so poorly, how was I supposed to know?

jokes.text: I'm glad you didn't feel you needed to clean up on my account, you know? It shows just how comfortable a friendship we actually have. / I did clean up. / Right. That's... what I meant.
jokes.setup: Johnny arrives at Stevie's apartment and observes (charitably) that she didn't clean up, interpreting it as comfort/friendship.
jokes.punchline: Stevie flatly reveals she DID clean up — meaning her 'clean' apartment is just... that messy. Johnny immediately tries to retroactively reframe his comment.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured three-beat joke. Johnny's diplomatic framing, Stevie's deadpan correction, and Johnny's clumsy recovery ('Right. That's... what I meant.') all land in sequence. The joke works because it simultaneously characterizes both — Johnny's tactfulness that backfires, Stevie's matter-of-fact self-awareness. The pause before 'what I meant' implies the visible awkwardness. Strong cringe comedy with warmth.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I haven't had a minute to myself since the baby was born. It's constant. I'm exhausted.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I don't even remember the last time I had any me time.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Well, I guess I get five, six nights a week when Felix takes the baby and I'm alone in my room, but that doesn't count.

jokes.text: Good. We bought the second motel!
jokes.setup: Stevie asks how things are at the motel, expecting small talk.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's immediate pivot from casual 'Good' to a major business announcement — 'We bought the second motel!' — with total enthusiasm, is a funny gear shift.
jokes.explanation: Functional plot-advancing joke. The casual-to-big-news transition is mildly amusing. Not especially crafted but lands in context.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I'm not ready for this premiere. I don't have anything clean to wear.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I've got dirty underwear piled up to the ceiling. Do I look like I have time to do laundry before tonight?

jokes.text: But not on your bed. / Oh, here.
jokes.setup: Johnny wants to sit and talk with Stevie about business. He surveys her apartment.
jokes.punchline: Apparently her apartment has no proper seating — just a bed — and Johnny pointedly does NOT want to sit on it, implying the awkwardness of sitting on a subordinate's bed.
jokes.explanation: A visual gag that works well — the stark apartment without furniture beyond a bed (implied by the rapid 'Oh, here' fix) says everything about Stevie's living situation. The awkwardness of boss potentially sitting on employee's bed is good comedy of manners. Efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I'll help you out, but I got conditions.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Of course you do. What are they?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I want the red carpet treatment. Full VIP situation.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ronnie, it's the opening of a new Subway in Schitt's Creek.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: And? I still deserve to be treated like royalty.

jokes.text: You don't have to downplay your excitement on my account, Stevie. I'll be just fine.
jokes.setup: Stevie has received Johnny's gift (his book, apparently) with muted, polite thanks rather than genuine excitement.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's resigned acceptance that Stevie is underwhelmed — but framed as him being generous and self-sacrificing — is quietly funny given his evident expectation that she'd be thrilled.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's barely-concealed disappointment masked as magnanimity is a warm, understated joke. Works on the character level — his ego as an author needs acknowledgment, which he doesn't get.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I'm going first. I don't care what Roland's doing or what time he's showing up. I'm upstaging that man, and I'm going first.

jokes.text: Oh, it's barely noticeable, dear. We'll find you an electrologist.
jokes.setup: Alexis says 'Okay, so I may have a problem.' Moira's response suggests she's looking at something on Alexis's face/body.
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately assumes the problem is unwanted hair and offers to find an electrologist — a non sequitur that suggests Moira notices physical 'flaws' before anything else.
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection. 'Electrologist' is specific enough to be funny on its own, and Moira's instinct to go straight to physical appearance is perfect characterization. The fact that she offers this as comfort — 'barely noticeable' — while still clearly noting it is peak Moira mothering.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm having some technical difficulties.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You left instructions.

jokes.text: I may have been wrong and you may have been not wrong.
jokes.setup: Moira needs to concede that Alexis's premiere idea was actually good.
jokes.punchline: Rather than simply saying 'You were right,' Moira constructs the most convoluted possible non-admission: 'I may have been wrong and you may have been not wrong.'
jokes.explanation: This is a great Moira line — possibly the episode's most quotable. 'You may have been not wrong' is a masterclass in character-specific verbal construction: it simultaneously acknowledges Alexis's correctness while refusing to grant her the direct credit. The double hedge ('may have been') and the double negative ('not wrong') reflect Moira's entire ego architecture. High quotability, strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So what did the airline say about your application?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: They want to hire me as a pilot.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A pilot? Stevie, you don't know anything about flying planes.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I know. Apparently they called your reference and you told them I was 'excellent under pressure.'

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was talking about your ability to work the front desk during busy check-ins!

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, they're very excited to have me start next week.

jokes.text: When Demi decided on a Friday night at 6pm that she wanted to celebrate her 40th in Aspen, her publicist had us in parkas by 7. Sadly, Megan went into cardiac arrest on the flight, but she perished doing what she loved.
jokes.setup: Moira is illustrating what real event planning under pressure looks like with a celebrity anecdote.
jokes.punchline: The story ends with the publicist dying of cardiac arrest mid-flight, and Moira's consolation is 'she perished doing what she loved' — as though event-planning travel is a noble calling worth dying for.
jokes.explanation: A standout Moira monologue. The story is vintage Moira: celebrity name-drops ('Demi'), specificity (6pm, in parkas by 7), and then the dark turn — a publicist dying mid-flight — treated as an inspiring anecdote. 'She perished doing what she loved' is darkly hilarious and perfectly Moira's brand of casual tragedy. Matches the 8.0 anchor standard easily. One of the episode's best moments.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: His business card is just a napkin with his phone number written in crayon.

jokes.text: When Demi decided on a Friday night at 6pm that she wanted to celebrate her 40th in Aspen, her publicist had us in parkas by 7. Sadly, Megan went into cardiac arrest on the flight, but she perished doing what she loved.
jokes.setup: Moira is defending the idea of throwing together a premiere in four hours, citing industry examples.
jokes.punchline: Her Aspen anecdote ends with the publicist dying of cardiac arrest on the plane — which Moira frames as a positive ('she perished doing what she loved') and moves on from immediately.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best jokes. The structure is impeccable: glamorous celebrity story → increasingly implausible logistics → casual death → Moira's grotesquely positive spin. 'She perished doing what she loved' is a perfect dark punchline, and the fact that Moira pivots from it immediately (without registering any concern) is the darkest layer. Only Moira could use a colleague's death as a motivational anecdote. Very high quotability and shock value.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He told me I looked like Channing Tatum. And I said, 'Thank you.'

jokes.text: Um, is that your gown? What is that for? / Oh, I thought I might throw it on for tonight. / So you're going to the screening? / Yes. A simple 'Thank you, Mother' might be a nice place to start.
jokes.setup: Alexis notices Moira is already dressed in a gown — for the event Moira had previously refused to attend.
jokes.punchline: Moira not only plans to attend but expects gratitude from Alexis for this favor.
jokes.explanation: The dynamic between Moira's obliviousness and Alexis's exasperation is reliable comedy. 'A simple Thank you, Mother might be a nice place to start' is a good Moira line. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I picture things that don't happen all the time. Like, I'm married to Christina Aguilera.

jokes.text: Also, I thought you returned that [dress]? I did! Or I know I returned a dress. A glittering, backless thing. Stella McCartney, I think. That was mine. I've been looking for that. Blame UPS.
jokes.setup: Alexis questions why Moira has a gown she returned.
jokes.punchline: Moira casually reveals she returned Alexis's Stella McCartney dress instead of her own, discovered mid-conversation, and blames UPS.
jokes.explanation: The escalation within the exchange is well-paced: accusation → Moira's certainty → crack in certainty → description of the dress → Alexis's recognition → 'Blame UPS.' 'Blame UPS' as a deflection is a perfect Moira move — outsourcing responsibility to a shipping company for her own mix-up. Strong character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I think I've always wanted a baby, you know? It's just been this deep, primal thing inside me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: When did this start?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I watched 'Nine Months' with Hugh Grant in 1995.

jokes.text: I was in the middle of a pre-show bath, and with the baby around, I only get that kind of me-time maybe... five to six nights a week, so...
jokes.setup: Jocelyn answers Alexis's call, complaining about being interrupted from her bath.
jokes.punchline: She says she only gets bath-time 'five to six nights a week' — implying this is a sacrifice, when five to six nights a week is actually near-daily and quite generous.
jokes.explanation: Lovely character joke that works by the gap between what Jocelyn thinks is a relatable complaint (deprived of me-time!) and what she accidentally reveals (she has a bath almost every night). Her total lack of irony makes it funnier. Strong character integration — only Jocelyn would complain about 5-6 nights as if it were once a week.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why are you dressed like that?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Your mother insisted. Apparently, we're having a 'formal dinner.'

jokes.text: Oh, sanp! It's a premiere now? 'Cause first you said it was, and then you said it was just a viewing party.
jokes.setup: Alexis is updating Jocelyn on the event's status.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn says 'Oh, sanp!' — apparently her version of 'Oh, snap!' — which is either a character-specific mispronunciation or just her particular idiom.
jokes.explanation: 'Sanp' as a malapropism is a small but funny Jocelyn-specific detail. The confusion over the event's status (premiere vs. viewing party) mirrors the audience's own experience tracking Moira's decisions, which adds a meta layer.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, your pupils are absolutely enormous.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, maybe I'm just happy to see you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Don't be cute with me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not being cute. I'm being realistic.

jokes.text: Yeah, and, and if it's a premiere, we're all in trouble, because my skinny jeans are in the wash.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is weighing the implications of it being a real premiere.
jokes.punchline: Her biggest crisis: her skinny jeans are in the wash. This is her red-alert, and it reveals the smallness of her world with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Works as a fish-out-of-water/small-town joke. 'My skinny jeans are in the wash' as a red alert for a film premiere captures the gap between Jocelyn's world and Hollywood perfectly. Her sincerity elevates it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We're having a screening of 'The Crows Have Eyes 3: The Crowening.' It's the third installment in the franchise.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The Crow... en... ing.

jokes.text: All I've got is a 20-foot merlot in my garage. I ripped out after the church basement flooded.
jokes.setup: Alexis says she'll settle for any shade of red carpet, not necessarily 60 feet.
jokes.punchline: Roland has a 20-foot merlot-colored carpet in his garage that was salvaged from a flooded church basement — specific, absurd provenance that is perfectly Roland.
jokes.explanation: The specificity ('merlot,' 'church basement flooded') is everything here. Roland has a salvaged carpet from a church flood — of course he does. This is world-building comedy at its best.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The title is 'The Awakening: The Crowening'.

jokes.text: We have a 12-time Daytime Emmy Award-attending actress coming to walk a merlot carpet in hours.
jokes.setup: Alexis is building urgency about the premiere situation.
jokes.punchline: '12-time Daytime Emmy Award-ATTENDING' — not winning, not nominated, just attending. This is the precise language of someone who knows the difference and can't quite claim victory.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. 'Emmy Award-attending' is a brilliant distinction — it sounds like a credential but is actually a comprehensive non-achievement. Alexis (who handles her mother's PR) has clearly learned to reframe 'attended' as if it were 'nominated.' The specificity of 'merlot carpet' (not red — the specific shade) is also funny. This is sharp, character-specific, quietly devastating comedy writing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Caw! Caw! No, no, no—CAW! I'm trying to tell you I'm one of you!

jokes.text: and press coming from all over, bringing attention to your town. So either you can help me out or I pull the plug now and nobody wins.
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to convince Jocelyn to cooperate.
jokes.punchline: She characterizes national press attending as doing Schitt's Creek a favor — 'bringing attention to your town' — which is both true and condescending, and 'nobody wins' is an ultimatum that's barely threatening.
jokes.explanation: Functional dialogue that does character work but isn't especially crafted as a joke. The condescension is there but not particularly sharp.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, this is a disaster.

jokes.text: Wait, you wanna walk the carpet? / Honey, it's a movie premiere, is it not?
jokes.setup: After agreeing to help, Jocelyn makes her demand: she wants to walk the carpet.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's 'Honey, it's a movie premiere, is it not?' delivered with full self-assurance — as if her entitlement to red-carpet access is self-evident.
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's assumption of celebrity-adjacent privilege is funny and well-delivered. The 'Honey' adds a layer of confident entitlement. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Look, I thought they were crows, okay? They looked like crows!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, those are seagulls. Painted seagulls.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, how was I supposed to know that? They're all black and angry-looking!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The craftsmanship is actually quite impressive. Very architectural.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so I made one tiny mistake with some birds. It's not like I hurt anyone.

jokes.text: And I assume the mayor is walking the carpet as well, yes? / Yes... if he has to.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn negotiates her carpet time, also securing Roland's spot.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's resigned 'Yes... if he has to' is a perfect reluctant concession — as if Roland walking a carpet is a barely acceptable outcome.
jokes.explanation: The three-word hesitation 'Yes... if he has to' conveys everything about Alexis's feelings about Roland. Economy is excellent. Dependent on prior knowledge of Roland's character.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, the optics on a disaster film actually premiering during a disaster are... not ideal.

jokes.text: I would like to request a time before Roland. And I'd like to go first.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn and Alexis are scheduling carpet arrival times.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn wants to go before Roland (to avoid being associated with him); Alexis also wants to go first — a miniature scheduling power struggle that treats a small-town event like the Oscars.
jokes.explanation: Functional comedy of status anxiety — everyone wants premier placement on a 20-foot merlot carpet at a small-town movie night. Works more as character texture than as a sharp joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's a really solid concept, David. People love events.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, and people also love not being sued. Two great tastes that don't go together.

jokes.text: I think they're really gonna pop on your 20-foot merlot. I think so too! They're gonna pop on that carpet.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is excited about wearing her skinny jeans to the premiere.
jokes.punchline: Both Jocelyn and Alexis enthusiastically agree that the skinny jeans will 'pop' on the merlot carpet — treating a 20-foot piece of salvaged church-basement carpet as if it's the actual Met Gala.
jokes.explanation: Works as a warm, shared delusion between two characters who are equally earnest about a small-town event. 'Pop on that carpet' is funnier for the disproportion between confidence and context.

dialogue.speaker: News Reporter
dialogue.line: We're following breaking news out of the Schitt's Creek Drive-In where moviegoers attending the premiere of 'The Murder of Crows' were forced to evacuate after an unexpected murder of crows attacked the outdoor venue.

dialogue.speaker: News Reporter
dialogue.line: The irony of crow attacks occurring at a movie premiere about crow attacks is not lost on us.

jokes.text: Stevie! Perfect timing. I was having a technical issue opening a file here. It's, uh, being finicky. / Uh, remember, Mr. Rose? I left you a sticky note.
jokes.setup: Johnny is struggling with a computer file and greets Stevie as if she's arrived perfectly in time.
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals she left him a sticky note (instructions for the technical issue), implying this is a recurring problem and that his helplessness is well-documented.
jokes.explanation: Gentle joke about Johnny's tech incompetence and Stevie's long-suffering but prepared assistance. Characterful. The sticky note detail implies a whole history of this dynamic.

jokes.text: Ah! Boy, it's times like this I really miss having you around, Stevie.
jokes.setup: Johnny is ostensibly there to make a business proposal about buying Stevie out. He has just revealed he can't open a file.
jokes.punchline: His admission that he misses her specifically because she can fix his computer — while he's supposedly there to be magnanimous — is quietly funny.
jokes.explanation: Warm, functional character beat. The irony of missing someone for mundane reasons (file-opening) when emotional ones are available is mild but pleasant.

jokes.text: Is it Larry Air? 'Cause they called me for a reference, and also asked if I had any interest in becoming a pilot.
jokes.setup: Stevie says she's settled on something she wants. Johnny guesses.
jokes.punchline: Johnny guesses 'Larry Air' — a local airline apparently named after a Larry — and reveals they offered him a job as a pilot despite no pilot credentials being implied.
jokes.explanation: Absurdist small-town detail — a regional airline called 'Larry Air' that is so unprofessional it's recruiting people without pilot credentials. The image of Johnny as a potential pilot is funny. Good world-building comedy.

jokes.text: No. I'm talking about trusting your instincts. [beat] I think I've been taking them for granted.
jokes.setup: Johnny says learning to trust your instincts comes with age and experience. Stevie says she's talking about trusting HIS instincts — then there's a beat before she clarifies she's been taking them for granted.
jokes.punchline: The brief moment where Johnny thinks Stevie is saying she doesn't trust her OWN instincts (a touching admission) is subverted when she clarifies she means HIS instincts — a more deflating and funnier kind of trust.
jokes.explanation: A warm character beat that's also slightly funny — the pivot from 'trusting yourself' to 'trusting you' is a sweet redirection. Works better emotionally than comedically, but there's genuine humor in the beat.

jokes.text: Well, I... should probably tell you that you don't necessarily have to trust all my instincts... all the time. Since you've been gone, I did have to bring on a third business partner, and I may as well tell you that it's... Roland.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie have just had a warm, emotionally resonant reunion/recommitment scene. Johnny then begins an admission with 'I should probably tell you...'
jokes.punchline: The damning reveal: Roland is now a business partner. The pause before 'Roland' and the 'I may as well tell you that it's...' build the dread perfectly.
jokes.explanation: Perfect structure — the warmth of the reunion scene makes the Roland reveal funnier by contrast. Johnny's progressive hedging ('you don't necessarily have to trust all my instincts... all the time') before the reveal is excellent build-up. The pause before 'Roland' is the punchline, not even the word itself.

jokes.text: I know. He gave me his new business card. Never saw so many typos on a business card.
jokes.setup: Johnny has just revealed Roland is the new business partner.
jokes.punchline: Stevie already knows — Roland gave her a business card so riddled with typos that it's the thing she remembers most about it.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Roland-character joke delivered entirely in absentia. 'Never saw so many typos on a business card' is a perfect line — specific, unexpected, and the mental image of a business card with MULTIPLE typos says everything about Roland as a business partner. The economy is very strong. Stevie's deadpan acceptance is also characterful.

jokes.text: Do you mind if we don't talk about Roland right now, 'cause I'm feeling really good about this decision.
jokes.setup: Having just learned Roland is their business partner — with the typo-business-card as the icing on the cake.
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately requests a Roland moratorium to protect her enthusiasm for her decision — acknowledging that thinking about Roland too hard might reverse it.
jokes.explanation: Good button on the Roland revelation. The self-awareness that talking about Roland might undermine her decision is both funny and revealing about the Roland dynamic. Works very well in context.

jokes.text: Morning, sunshine. / Mm. It's 5pm. / But good morning.
jokes.setup: Patrick wakes up from post-anesthesia sleep.
jokes.punchline: David greets him with 'Morning, sunshine'; Patrick corrects the time; David's response — 'But good morning' — ignores the correction entirely.
jokes.explanation: 'But good morning' is a charming non sequitur — David acknowledges the correction and overrides it simultaneously, which is very him. Sweet and functional.

jokes.text: the conversation really ran the gamut. You complimented my flawless skin and called me the Jewish Channing Tatum, which all tracks.
jokes.setup: Patrick asks what he was saying while anesthetized.
jokes.punchline: David recounts being called 'the Jewish Channing Tatum' with total acceptance — 'which all tracks' — as if this is simply factual information Patrick has now confirmed.
jokes.explanation: 'The Jewish Channing Tatum, which all tracks' is an exceptional David Rose line. The specificity of the comparison, combined with David's complete lack of irony in confirming it as factual, is perfectly character-specific. David's self-regard, delivered as simple reporting rather than bragging, is the joke. High quotability — this is a line fans will repeat.

jokes.text: I put you to sleep after that. / Wait. Are you, are you joking? / No. No, no. You held me close and told me to envision our lives... with a child.
jokes.setup: David tells Patrick he wanted to have a baby — Patrick is alarmed.
jokes.punchline: David's dramatic, slow delivery of 'envision our lives... with a child' recounts a very odd request from an anesthetized Patrick with maximum gravity — as if reporting a ghost sighting.
jokes.explanation: Works well as a callback — the gap between the sweetness of the earlier moment and David's serious, slightly scandalized reporting of it is funny. David's dramatized pause before 'with a child' is a performance choice that lands.

jokes.text: That, uh, that must've been scary for you. / It was a little triggering. Traumatizing.
jokes.setup: Patrick asks if the baby conversation was scary for David.
jokes.punchline: David's escalation from 'triggering' to 'traumatizing' — as if being told someone wants a baby with you is a psychological event — is hilariously self-centered but also reveals David's genuine terror of the concept.
jokes.explanation: David's use of therapeutic language ('triggering,' 'traumatizing') for what is a romantic expression of care is characteristically David — co-opting wellness vocabulary for maximum drama. The escalation from one word to another is concise and funny.

jokes.text: I'm not married to Christina Aguilera, am I?
jokes.setup: David asks if Patrick has ever pictured himself doing something that didn't happen.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's example of an unfulfilled fantasy: not being married to Christina Aguilera. The randomness and specificity of this particular celebrity, delivered with perfect deadpan, is the entire joke.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best jokes. The choice of Christina Aguilera is specific enough to be bizarre, pop-culturally resonant enough to land, and delivered with such perfect nonchalance that it achieves maximum comedy. It also does character work — it implies Patrick has a type (bold, expressive, possibly gay-icon-adjacent), which has meta-resonance for a character who was in the closet. Extremely quotable. The economy ('am I?') is perfect.

jokes.text: I don't wanna undermine the significance of this conversation, but I do wanna make it known that I was watching 'Bridget Jones's Baby' right before I went under.
jokes.setup: David is taking the baby conversation seriously, wanting to make sure Patrick isn't suppressing real desires.
jokes.punchline: Patrick undermines the entire emotional conversation by revealing the actual source of his baby feelings: 'Bridget Jones's Baby' on TV before anesthesia.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback and structural resolution to the baby arc. The revelation that the emotional throughline of the episode was triggered by a rom-com is both deflating and very funny. 'Right before I went under' as the timing makes the causal chain even more absurd. The honesty and delivery sell it completely.

jokes.text: Okay, well that makes a little more sense then. / Mm. You would be a great dad, though.
jokes.setup: David accepts the Bridget Jones's Baby explanation with relief.
jokes.punchline: Patrick immediately adds 'You would be a great dad, though' — which partially reinstates the sentiment David thought he'd escaped, with a warm afterglow.
jokes.explanation: A nice button — Patrick can't completely take it back, and the fact that he still means it (sincerely, not from the movie this time) gives the moment warmth and mild humor simultaneously.

jokes.text: Um... Can you grab me those painkillers, please? I think I'm gonna need a few of them if we're still planning on making it to your mom's thing tonight. / Okay, that's actually a very good idea. I might take one myself. / Apparently the first 15 minutes are very, very dense.
jokes.setup: The couple is planning to attend Moira's premiere tonight.
jokes.punchline: David decides to preemptively take painkillers for the premiere; he justifies it by recalling Moira's own warning that 'the first third is very dense exposition.' The callback ties the two storylines together comedically.
jokes.explanation: Smart structural callback — Moira's own damning review of her film is now weaponized as justification for pain medication before watching it. The two storylines colliding is satisfying and the joke earns its payoff. David preemptively medicating for his mother's movie is classic.

jokes.text: No, John, tonight is special, and I'd like my hand-candy by my side.
jokes.setup: John says he'll be 10 feet behind Moira on the carpet (deferent to her moment).
jokes.punchline: Moira calls John her 'hand-candy' — a gender-flipped version of 'arm candy' that is a characteristically Moira coinage.
jokes.explanation: 'Hand-candy' is a perfect Moira-ism — a slight twist on a familiar phrase that feels like it came from a slightly different universe. The character integration is exceptional; it's simultaneously a compliment to John and a categorization of him as an accessory. Warm and funny.

jokes.text: Why are your pupils so dilated. / No, yours are. / No, yours are.
jokes.setup: The Rose children and Patrick arrive at the premiere. Alexis clocks that David and Patrick are acting oddly.
jokes.punchline: Rather than explaining, both David and Patrick deflect by accusing Alexis of the same thing — a childish double-deflection that implies everyone is mildly under some influence.
jokes.explanation: The playground-level 'no, yours are' deflection from two adults is funny and reveals their complicity. Sets up the drug-adjacent humor without being explicit about it.

jokes.text: Why are you dressed like a limo driver? / Your mother insisted.
jokes.setup: Alexis notices John's outfit.
jokes.punchline: 'Your mother insisted' — the resigned acceptance of Moira's aesthetic demands, and the fact that Moira apparently hired her own husband as a limo driver-costumed escort.
jokes.explanation: Three words do the work of a whole scene. 'Your mother insisted' as an explanation for anything outlandish is a reliable running joke in the series. The visual of John in a limo driver outfit is funny.

jokes.text: Why are your pupils so dilated. / No, yours are. / No, yours are.
jokes.setup: David and Patrick arrive at the premiere, both having taken painkillers.
jokes.punchline: Each accuses the other of having dilated pupils — neither realizing they're both equally impaired, creating a perfectly symmetrical mutual obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of both of them taking painkillers is well-handled — the symmetry of each accusing the other is the right structure. Visual comedy implied by their shared state.

jokes.text: Alexis, Mommy's home now. I'll take it from here.
jokes.setup: Alexis is managing the premiere logistics and trying to maintain control.
jokes.punchline: Moira takes over with 'Mommy's home now' — infantilizing Alexis while asserting dominance with a warm maternal metaphor that's actually quite cutting.
jokes.explanation: 'Mommy's home now' is a quotable Moira line — it's simultaneously sweet and devastating, which is Moira's genius. The juxtaposition of childlike phrasing with a total power grab is characteristically her. Strong quotability.

jokes.text: Ladies and gentlemen, it is my great pleasure to welcome you to a North American screening of... the psychological thriller... 'The Crows Have Eyes III: The Crowening!'
jokes.setup: Moira takes the microphone to introduce her film.
jokes.punchline: She describes a crow horror sequel as 'the psychological thriller' with full gravitas, and the title 'The Crowening' (a crow pun on 'The Reckoning' / 'The Shining') lands as both an awful pun and a brilliant piece of in-universe absurdity.
jokes.explanation: 'The Crowening' is a magnificent title — the joke is that it's simultaneously a terrible pun and exactly the kind of movie Moira would be in, delivered with complete sincerity. Moira calling it 'a psychological thriller' elevates it beyond horror. High quotability for the title alone.

jokes.text: Ladies and gentlemen, it is my great pleasure to welcome you to a North American screening of... the psychological thriller... Move it along. 'The Crows Have Eyes III: The Crowening!'
jokes.setup: Moira gives a grand introduction to the film, interrupted by Alexis.
jokes.punchline: 'The Crowening' as a subtitle — and the juxtaposition of 'psychological thriller' with the crow-pun subtitle — is inherently absurd. Plus Moira ignoring Alexis's 'move it along' and going full theatrical.
jokes.explanation: 'The Crowening' is a great title joke — it's the kind of pun that a low-budget horror franchise would actually use. Moira's theatrical delivery of what is objectively a terrible title is the source of comedy.

jokes.text: Please, everyone stay calm! Whatever you do, do not look them in the eye! / Okay, you're making them angrier! I'm not, Alexis! I know a bit of their language. CAW! CAW-CAW!
jokes.setup: Crows have been released and are apparently attacking people at the premiere.
jokes.punchline: Amid genuine chaos, Moira attempts to de-escalate by speaking crow — 'I know a bit of their language' — and producing 'CAW! CAW-CAW!' with complete confidence.
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic peak and one of the show's great absurdist moments. 'I know a bit of their language' followed by 'CAW! CAW-CAW!' is a perfect escalation of Moira's self-regard into total absurdity. The character integration is near-perfect — only Moira would: (a) believe she knows crow language, (b) deploy it publicly, (c) do so with complete sincerity. This is exceptional television comedy: visual chaos + perfect character behavior. Very high impact, extremely quotable, will be rewatched.

jokes.text: I know a bit of their language. CAW! CAW-CAW!
jokes.setup: Crows are attacking. Alexis tells Moira she's making it worse.
jokes.punchline: Moira insists she knows 'a bit of their language' and begins CAW-ing at the crows — which presumably makes the situation worse.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic peak. Moira claiming she knows 'a bit of their language' during a crow attack, then CAW-ing with theatrical commitment, is perfect absurdist character comedy. The confidence with which she attempts crow communication — and the implication that it makes things worse — is both very funny and entirely consistent with Moira's character. High originality, excellent character integration. Impact is 9 because this would get the room.

jokes.text: This is fucked.
jokes.setup: Crows are attacking premiere attendees. Chaos reigns.
jokes.punchline: David's flat, precise three-word assessment of the situation.
jokes.explanation: The perfect reaction beat — amid absurdist chaos, David's flat expletive is the straight-man punctuation. Maximum economy (literally three words), but the joke is situational rather than crafted. Works entirely because of context and David's delivery.

jokes.text: The worst part is people are being robbed of the experience of seeing the film on the big screen.
jokes.setup: People have fled the premiere due to the crow attack.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'worst part' framing — after a genuine public safety incident — is that people missed the theatrical experience of her film.
jokes.explanation: Moira's complete inability to prioritize human safety over her film's theatrical experience is darkly funny and perfectly character-specific. The phrase 'robbed of the experience' is pompous and wrong in this context. Very strong character integration.

jokes.text: No, that's not the worst part, Moira.
jokes.setup: Moira says the worst part is people missing the big-screen experience.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat correction — delivered in his reliable long-suffering partner mode — is the straight-man beat that validates the audience's reaction.
jokes.explanation: Necessary straight-man beat, but not a joke in itself — it exists to heighten Moira's moment. Economy is the only real virtue. Functional rather than funny.

jokes.text: Tonight, a murder of wild crows attacked would-be audience members who had come to see a movie about a crow attack. We must warn you, the following content is pretty graphic.
jokes.setup: A news clip about the premiere chaos is shown.
jokes.punchline: The news report perfectly encapsulates the absurdity: real crows attacked people who came to see a fictional crow attack. The use of 'murder' (technically correct collective noun for crows) adds a pun layer. 'Pretty graphic' as a news warning for crow chaos is mildly understated.
jokes.explanation: Elegant structural callback — the news report is the objective summary of the episode's comic premise. 'A murder of wild crows attacked would-be audience members who came to see a movie about a crow attack' is genuinely excellent writing — layered, ironic, uses the technical term 'murder' (crow collective noun) as double meaning, and frames the whole absurdity perfectly.

jokes.text: Okay, they weren't 'wild' crows. They were supposed to be trained. Trained! Some of them were painted seagulls.
jokes.setup: The news has called the crows 'wild.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis defends the operation — they were 'trained' — then immediately undermines this by revealing some were painted seagulls. The escalation from 'this is a defense' to 'this makes it worse' is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. 'Some of them were painted seagulls' is one of the most purely absurd lines in the episode. The structure is flawless: outrage at being mischaracterized → attempt to defend → revelation that makes the situation far worse. Who PAINTS seagulls to look like crows for a movie premiere? The image is so specific and bizarre that it achieves perfect absurdist comedy. Very high quotability — this is the kind of detail audiences will remember and quote for years.

jokes.text: It sorta looks like people are running away from your movie, Moira.
jokes.setup: The news video shows people fleeing the premiere.
jokes.punchline: David's dry observation that the footage looks like the film's reception rather than a crow attack — though he's careful to say 'sorta looks like.'
jokes.explanation: A solid twist of the knife from David — the meta-reading of the chaos footage as a metaphor for box office failure. 'Sorta looks like' is a careful hedge that makes it funnier (he's being precise about his observation). Good dark humor.

jokes.text: You! Look what you have done! / Look what I've done? This was your idea, and now I have to pay Ronnie for damage to her carpet! / What were the crows even doing there in the first place? / It's called an immersive experience, David! / Immersed in lawsuits maybe.
jokes.setup: The premiere has dissolved into chaos and blame-assignment begins.
jokes.punchline: David's 'Immersed in lawsuits maybe' is the punchline — a perfectly calibrated one-liner that turns Alexis's PR language against her.
jokes.explanation: 'Immersed in lawsuits maybe' is one of the episode's best pure joke lines. It's a perfect callback to the 'immersive experience' concept, delivered with David's characteristic verbal precision. The blame-trading between Moira and Alexis is also funny as a chaotic button on the whole premiere arc. David's interjection cuts through perfectly.

jokes.text: Alexis, make it stop!
jokes.setup: The news video continues to play, showing the premiere disaster.
jokes.punchline: Moira's plaintive 'Alexis, make it stop!' — addressed to her publicist daughter — is a helpless, childlike moment from a woman who was moments ago commanding crow language.
jokes.explanation: A good final button — Moira collapses from imperious matriarch to helpless child in one line. Works as the episode's closing comedic beat. The juxtaposition with her recent crow-language performance makes it funnier.

tmdb_overview: Alexis plans a local premiere for the "Crows" movie, and Moira makes a big splash on the red carpet. David tends to Patrick after his wisdom teeth removal.
insight: Absurdist chaos edges toward elite: 57 jokes in 25 minutes, just shy of greatness.
